ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for balsamic vinegar as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for condiments is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of balsamic vinegar is approximately 1 tablespoon (15 g), used as a salad dressing, condiment, or finishing drizzle.

nutrient_source.title: Balsamic Vinegar — USDA FoodData Central, FDC ID 173469 (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for balsamic vinegar used as primary nutrient reference. Input HPB values for energy (88 kcal), protein (0.49 g), fat (0.0 g), carbohydrate (17.03 g), sugar (14.95 g), sodium (23 mg), potassium (112 mg), calcium (27 mg), and iron (0.72 mg) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data and are retained. USDA SR Legacy confirms fat is 0.0 g (naturally zero — balsamic vinegar contains no fat by composition). Saturated fat and dietary fibre are confirmed at 0.0 g per USDA SR Legacy. Micronutrient fields beyond those reported in the primary HPB and USDA SR Legacy data are set to -1 as detailed in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for balsamic vinegar was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of approximately 25 is estimated based on: (1) vinegar's well-documented acetic acid content which suppresses post-prandial glucose response; (2) the GI of grape juice/must analogues (GI ~45–55) substantially reduced by acetic acid fermentation; (3) peer-reviewed evidence (Johnston et al., 2004, JADA; Brighenti et al., 1995, European Journal of Clinical Nutrition) demonstrating that acetic acid in vinegar reduces the GI of co-consumed foods and has an intrinsic low GI. GL per serving = (25 × 2.55 g available carbohydrate per 15 g serving) / 100 = 0.64, rounded to 0.6. This is a conservative low-GI estimate consistent with published vinegar GI literature.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (23 mg/100 g); sugar content (14.95 g/100 g) is clinically negligible at typical condiment serving sizes of 5–15 ml.
hypertension.guidance: Balsamic vinegar is suitable for people with hypertension when used in typical condiment quantities (1–2 teaspoons per serving), as its sodium content is very low and actual sugar intake per use is minimal; avoid large-volume use as a glaze or marinade base.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~25) due to acetic acid content; sugar is 14.95 g/100 g but actual intake at typical serving sizes (5–15 ml) is only 0.75–2.25 g, which is clinically negligible; acetic acid has evidence for attenuating post-prandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Balsamic vinegar used in small condiment quantities (1–2 teaspoons) is appropriate for people with Type 2 Diabetes, as its acetic acid content may help moderate post-meal blood glucose; avoid large quantities as a glaze or marinade where sugar load becomes significant.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, very low sodium; grape-derived polyphenols (resveratrol, quercetin, anthocyanins) have documented cardioprotective antioxidant properties.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Balsamic vinegar is a heart-friendly condiment with no fat, no cholesterol, and very low sodium; its grape-derived polyphenols may offer cardioprotective benefits, and it can be used freely as a salad dressing or finishing condiment in a cardiovascular-protective diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Grape-derived polyphenols (resveratrol, quercetin, anthocyanins) with documented anti-inflammatory properties; acetic acid supports gut microbiome health relevant to immune regulation in RA; zero trans fat and no pro-inflammatory factors at typical serving sizes.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Balsamic vinegar used in small quantities as a condiment or dressing is appropriate for people with rheumatoid arthritis; choose traditional aged balsamic (Aceto Balsamico Tradizionale) for higher polyphenol content over commercial sweetened varieties.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Balsamic vinegar (Aceto Balsamico) is a dark brown, syrupy condiment originating from Modena and Reggio Emilia in northern Italy. It is produced by cooking and concentrating freshly pressed grape must (predominantly Trebbiano and Lambrusco grapes), which is then aged in a series of progressively smaller wooden barrels — typically chestnut, cherry, oak, mulberry, and ash — for a minimum of 12 years for traditional Denominazione di Origine Protetta (DOP) varieties. The result is a complex, sweet-tart liquid with notes of fig, molasses, cherry, and chocolate, and a characteristic thick, glossy texture. Commercially produced balsamic vinegar of Modena (IGP) uses a shorter aging process and may include wine vinegar and caramel colouring. Nutritionally, balsamic vinegar provides 88 kcal per 100 g, with 17.03 g carbohydrate (of which 14.95 g is sugar), 0.49 g protein, and zero fat. Sodium is very low at 23 mg/100 g. It contains grape-derived polyphenols including resveratrol, quercetin, and anthocyanins with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Acetic acid, the primary organic acid, has peer-reviewed evidence for attenuating post-prandial blood glucose response, making balsamic vinegar a low-GI condiment (estimated GI ~25) despite its sugar content. At typical serving sizes of 5–15 ml (1–3 teaspoons), actual sugar and calorie intake are clinically negligible. Balsamic vinegar is suitable for hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease management when used in condiment quantities. It is vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Is balsamic vinegar good for diabetics?
How much sugar is in balsamic vinegar?
Can I use balsamic vinegar if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between balsamic vinegar and balsamic glaze?
How many calories in balsamic vinegar?
Does balsamic vinegar lower blood sugar?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173469), an approved primary source, with corroboration from Singapore HPB input data. Values are consistent across both sources. GI is an analogous estimate based on peer-reviewed vinegar GI literature, clearly documented.
notes: fat_g is naturally zero — balsamic vinegar is produced from grape must and wine vinegar and contains no fat by composition; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173469). saturated_fat_g (0.0), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.0), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.0), trans_fat_g (0.0), and cholesterol_mg (0.0) are all confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173469. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 g, confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173469. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed micronutrient data for these nutrients are not reported for balsamic vinegar in the Singapore HPB database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173469) as of 2026: vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg. These micronutrients are either not measured or not published in the approved sources consulted; the USDA SR Legacy entry for balsamic vinegar (FDC ID 173469) does not include values for these fields. water_g of 76.45 g is sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173469. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate based on peer-reviewed vinegar acetic acid literature; no direct GI measurement for balsamic vinegar exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Balsamic vinegar is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; therefore no mandatory local-language names apply under G6 multilingual rules. The romanized Italian name 'Aceto Balsamico' is provided as a culturally relevant alias. Kosher status is marked false as traditional balsamic vinegar requires rabbinical certification which is not universally applicable.
